# Scam Compound Pattern Explorer in Southeast Asia

## Project Summary

This project develops a Google Earth Engine application to explore spatial patterns associated with reported scam compounds in Southeast Asia. Focusing on Cambodia, Myanmar and border-linked corridors, the application combines reported site data with Sentinel-2 spectral indicators, night-time lights, temporal change metrics and candidate areas produced by satellite-embedding similarity analysis. The tool is designed as an exploratory decision-support interface: it highlights areas with similar remote-sensing characteristics to confirmed sites, while avoiding claims of definitive site identification.

### Problem Statement

Scam compounds are difficult to monitor systematically because verified ground data are limited, sites can develop quickly, and suspicious compounds often resemble legitimate industrial, commercial or urban developments. A spatial application can help users compare reported sites, satellite indicators and candidate areas in one interface, making uncertainty and spatial context easier to interpret.

### End User

The application is designed for human-rights researchers, investigative journalists, policy analysts and academic users studying transnational crime in Southeast Asia. These users need a transparent way to inspect reported locations, compare spatial indicators, and identify areas that may warrant further investigation without treating model output as verified evidence.

### Data

The project uses reported scam-site points classified as confirmed, suspected or control locations. Remote-sensing inputs include Sentinel-2 surface reflectance imagery for RGB visualization, NDVI for vegetation, NDBI for built-up intensity, Google Satellite Embedding for similarity screening, and VIIRS night-time lights as a proxy for human activity. Candidate outputs are generated from similarity-based analysis and include priority tiers, temporal changes and distance metrics.

### Methodology

Confirmed sites are used as reference samples. The workflow extracts satellite embedding signatures from selected confirmed sites, applies a high-percentile similarity threshold, converts matched pixels into candidate points, buffers candidates into site-scale zones, and calculates NDVI, NDBI, night-time lights and distance metrics. Temporal metrics such as delta NDVI, delta NDBI and delta night-time lights describe development change. Spatial context, including border proximity and distance to confirmed sites, supports interpretation.

### Interface

The interface is map-first. Users can switch between regional AOIs, toggle satellite and indicator layers, show confirmed, suspected and control points, and inspect candidate areas by high, medium or low priority. Clicking on the map samples local NDVI, NDBI and night-time lights, reports the nearest known site, and displays candidate metrics when the selected location falls inside a candidate zone. This design supports cautious exploration rather than binary classification.

## The Application

Replace the placeholder below with the published Earth Engine App URL.

:::{.column-page}

<iframe src='YOUR_EARTH_ENGINE_APP_URL_HERE' width='100%' height='700px'></iframe>

:::

## How it Works

The application loads reported scam-site points and final candidate areas from Earth Engine assets, filters them by site status and priority, and defines regional AOIs for Cambodia-Vietnam, Myanmar-Thailand and the Golden Triangle. Sentinel-2 imagery is cloud-masked and composited by year. NDVI and NDBI are calculated from the composite, while VIIRS night-time lights are averaged annually.

```js
var scamPoints = ee.FeatureCollection(
  'projects/project-2736c40e-7bac-492d-b63/assets/scam_sites'
);

var candidates = ee.FeatureCollection(
  'users/houm4ki/0025analysis/Final_Summary_Table_Complete'
);

var confirmed = scamPoints.filter(ee.Filter.eq('site_status', 'confirmed'));
var suspected = scamPoints.filter(ee.Filter.eq('site_status', 'suspected'));

var aoiCambodiaVietnam = ee.Geometry.Rectangle([102.0, 10.0, 108.5, 15.5]);
```

Spectral indicators are calculated from Sentinel-2 bands. NDVI captures vegetation presence, while NDBI captures built-up intensity. Comparing these indicators across years helps describe potential development patterns.

```js
var ndvi = s2Recent.normalizedDifference(['B8', 'B4']).rename('NDVI');
var ndbi = s2Recent.normalizedDifference(['B11', 'B8']).rename('NDBI');
var indicators = ndvi.addBands(ndbi);
```

The similarity workflow uses the Google Satellite Embedding annual image collection. Candidate pixels are selected using a percentile threshold, converted into centroid candidates, buffered into 500 m zones and ranked by development and activity flags.

```js
var embeddings = ee.ImageCollection('GOOGLE/SATELLITE_EMBEDDING/V1/ANNUAL');
var meanSimilarity = similarityImages.mean().rename('similarity');
var similarPixels = meanSimilarity.gte(thresholdValue).selfMask();
```

The final visualization script adds an interactive control panel, layer toggles, summary charts, candidate priority layers and map-click inspection.

## Limitations

The application does not verify whether any candidate site is a scam compound. Confirmed locations are limited, suspected sites are uncertain, and similar spatial patterns may occur in legitimate industrial or commercial areas. Results should therefore be interpreted as indicators for further investigation rather than proof.

## References

- Google Earth Engine Data Catalog: Sentinel-2 Surface Reflectance Harmonized.
- Google Earth Engine Data Catalog: VIIRS Nighttime Day/Night Band Composites.
- Reported site sources listed in the project point dataset.
